author: Dr. Lizard
title: What mechanisms lead to biallelic TP53 inactivation in myelodysplastic syndrome?
answer: Biallelic inactivation occurs via two mutations, mutation plus deletion, copy-neutral LOH, or VAF over 50%
detail: Two distinct TP53 mutations on different alleles is the first mechanism of biallelic inactivation.
One mutation plus deletion of the wild-type allele at 17p is the second mechanism.
One mutation plus copy-neutral loss of heterozygosity at 17p is the third mechanism.
VAF exceeding 50% for a single mutation implies homozygosity and counts as biallelic inactivation.
releaseNote: This card summarizes the mechanisms leading to biallelic TP53 inactivation in myelodysplastic syndrome, including two distinct mutations, mutation plus deletion, copy-neutral LOH, and VAF over 50%
